# MACRO REPORT — 2026-04-21
**Delivered:** 11:45 UTC | **Generated by:** Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The dominant macro narrative entering Tuesday's session is the volatile US–Iran conflict, which has produced sharp whipsaws in oil, equities, and safe-haven assets over the past 72 hours. Brent crude has shed 5.27% today to $90.45 after surging above $96 on Sunday when Iran reimposed the Strait of Hormuz blockade following a US naval intercept, only to retreat as investors focused on renewed peace-talk prospects [Source: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/21/business/oil-stocks-gas-iran.html]. The single most important variable for today's session is the evolving Hormuz/ceasefire situation — any diplomatic breakthrough or further military escalation will immediately reprice energy, bonds, equities, and FX. Gold has stabilised at $4,806.70 as inflation fears vie with safe-haven demand, while Kevin Warsh's upcoming Fed confirmation hearing looms as the next major fixed-income catalyst [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-19/kevin-warsh-fed-hearings-will-be-treasury-bonds-next-catalyst].

---

## 1. GLOBAL MACRO OVERVIEW

Global markets are navigating a complex, conflict-driven macro environment in which the US–Iran war — now in its eighth week — continues to dictate the pulse of risk assets. Equity indices have, remarkably, recouped their war-onset losses, with the S&P 500 at 7,109.14 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/] and the DAX pushing to 24,569.83 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], but analysts are warning that further upside is contingent on corporate guidance rather than backward-looking earnings beats. According to CNBC, global stocks clawed back losses as investors rapidly unwound geopolitical risk hedges and refocused on AI-driven earnings momentum, with the rebound described as "a rapid unwind of the war-risk premium… rather than a fundamental reset" [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/21/global-stocks-iran-conflict-ai-rally.html].

On the inflation and monetary policy front, the US CPI stands at 3.32% YoY as of March [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL] and the Fed Funds Effective Rate is 3.64% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FEDFUNDS], with the 10-year Treasury yielding 4.26% and the 2-year at 3.71% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10]. The 55 basis point inversion between the policy rate ceiling and the 2-year reflects market pricing that includes at least one additional cut by year-end, a view reinforced when the brief Hormuz reopening last week caused the 2-year to dip back below 3.75% [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-19/kevin-warsh-fed-hearings-will-be-treasury-bonds-next-catalyst]. However, renewed oil price pressure and a PPI MoM of 1.78% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PPIACO] keep the Fed in a difficult position, balancing stagflationary pressure against weakening global growth.

Earnings season is adding an additional layer of complexity. This week sees 94 S&P 500 companies report, with Tesla, Boeing, UnitedHealth, and Intel as key names. The blended Q1 earnings growth rate currently stands at 13.1% YoY, fractionally below the 13.2% forecast at quarter-end, and while 88% of reporters have beaten consensus estimates, strategists at Bloomberg warn that forward guidance — not past results — will determine whether the market can sustain its record levels [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-18/strong-earnings-results-can-t-save-stocks-from-dimming-forecasts]. Investors will be especially vigilant for energy cost commentary.

The IMF–World Bank Spring Meetings concluded last week dominated by the Middle East war's energy price and supply shocks. The two institutions pledged a combined $150 billion in new financing for developing economies and warned that even a swift end to the conflict will weigh on global growth and drive up inflation given the cumulative damage already done to commodity supply chains [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/imf-world-bank-meetings-show-limits-mitigating-shocks-reliance-us-solutions-2026-04-19/]. Canada's CPI rose to 2.4% YoY in March, partially driven by a 21.2% monthly surge in gasoline prices linked to the war [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-20/canadas-annual-cpi-rises-24-iran-war-drives-gasoline-costs], illustrating how the conflict is propagating globally through the energy channel.

---

## 2. CONFLICT / GEOPOLITICAL STATUS

**US–IRAN WAR / STRAIT OF HORMUZ**

- Iran reimposed controls on the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, blaming the United States for "breaches of trust," triggering a surge in WTI to $90.17 (+7.5%) and Brent to $96.27 (+6.5%) on Sunday evening. [Source: https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/20/trump-administration-news-updates-today]
- The USS Spruance fired on an Iranian-flagged cargo ship attempting to evade the US naval blockade in the Gulf of Oman. Iran's military warned it would "retaliate against this U.S. armed piracy." [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/sectors/energy/articles/oil-prices-rise-iran-restricts-223336940.html]
- Oil prices pulled back Tuesday as investors shifted focus to a potential second round of peace talks between the US and Iran, with Brent retreating to approximately $95/barrel by Tuesday morning. [Source: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/21/business/oil-stocks-gas-iran.html]
- Analysts at Oil & Gas 360 predict the Strait of Hormuz could safely reopen within 3–4 weeks, but note that natural gas prices remain significantly elevated and further spikes are expected following April lows. [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/sectors/energy/articles/energy-market-assessment-predictions-strait-213026152.html]
- The IMF and World Bank have warned that the war's cumulative energy price shock will weigh on global growth even if conflict ends soon, with developing nations especially vulnerable. [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/battle-scarred-developing-nations-look-path-out-permacrisis-2026-04-20/]
- Kevin Warsh's upcoming Fed confirmation hearing is the next major domestic catalyst, with bond traders watching for signals on Fed independence, the balance sheet, and inflation tolerance under a potential new chair. [Source: https://www.axios.com/2026/04/20/key-questions-for-fed-chair-nominee]
- Global shipping supply chains remain broadly impaired, with Port of LA officials warning "there's nothing that's not at risk" as the blockade disrupts commodity flows. [Source: https://www.seatrade-maritime.com/containers/strait-of-hormuz-fears-only-part-of-wider-global-trade-risk-pola-hears]

## 3. FOREIGN EXCHANGE

### Major Pairs

**EUR/USD — ~1.1767** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- The euro continues to recover against the dollar, gaining 0.22% today as a brief de-escalation narrative supported risk sentiment and pressured the DXY. [Source: https://www.bitget.com/amp/news/detail/12560605376249]

- Catalyst: Kevin Warsh Fed confirmation hearing — hawkish Warsh rhetoric could boost USD and pressure EUR/USD lower

- Key levels: 1.3380 support | 1.3650 resistance
- Catalyst: UK CPI and labour market data due later this week; Hormuz escalation a key downside risk

- USD/JPY is broadly flat (+0.01%) and remains elevated as the BoJ holds ultra-accommodative policy while the Fed remains on hold. - Key levels: 1.3580 support | 1.3780 resistance
- Catalyst: Bank of Canada rate decision; crude oil price volatility linked to Hormuz developments

- USD/CHF fell 0.52%, the largest mover among G10 pairs today, as the Swiss franc continued to attract safe-haven flows amid unresolved Middle East tensions. The pair is trading at historically compressed levels, reflecting persistent CHF demand. [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/]

- NZD/USD led G10 gains today with a 0.76% advance, recovering from conflict-driven weakness. The kiwi benefits from improved risk appetite when peace-talk narratives dominate, given New Zealand's commodity export exposure. [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/]

## 5. GLOBAL EQUITY INDICES

### US Equities

**S&P 500 — ~7,109.14** | Bias: Neutral
The S&P 500 declined a modest 0.24% on Monday to 7,109.14 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], absorbing weekend Iran escalation news relatively well. The index has recovered to near its all-time high as investors rapidly unwound geopolitical hedges when ceasefire optimism emerged; however, CNBC notes the index "blew past 7,000 in an epic comeback rally" and analysts debate whether it can continue without a sustained energy price resolution [Source: https://www.marketwatch.com/livecoverage/s-p-500-dow-jones-nasdaq-oil-prices-war-in-iran-strait-of-hormuz-opened-closed]. With 88% of S&P 500 reporters beating consensus and blended Q1 earnings growth at 13.1% YoY [Source: https://www.forbes.com/sites/bill_stone/2026/04/19/tesla-leads-a-busy-earnings-week-with-94-sp-500-reports/], the earnings bar is high going into this week's 94-company reporting slate.

**Nasdaq 100 — ~26,590.34** | Bias: Neutral
The Nasdaq 100 fell 0.31% to 26,590.34 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], slightly underperforming the broader market as higher yields weigh on growth multiples. Tesla earnings this week are the key event, with Jefferies raising their price target to $350 from $300 [Source: https://www.tipranks.com/news/these-are-the-questions-that-tesla-tsla-will-answer-during-its-q1-earnings-call-this-week]. Goldman Sachs also flagged that Micron is driving over half of the S&P 500's earnings revisions, highlighting continued semiconductor sector leadership.

**Dow Jones — ~49,442.56** | Bias: Neutral
The Dow was virtually unchanged, falling just 0.01% to 49,442.56 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], outperforming Nasdaq as the more value-oriented index benefits from the energy sector's elevated profitability. Boeing reports this week following a 57% sales jump last quarter, with results before the open on Wednesday [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/19/earnings-playbook-tsla-ba-lead-a-big-week-of-reports.html].

### Asia

**Nikkei 225 — ~59,349.17** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
The Nikkei advanced 0.89% to 59,349.17 today [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], outperforming as peace-talk optimism boosted Asian markets. Japan remains highly exposed to Middle East energy prices given its near-total reliance on oil imports, so ceasefire progression is especially positive for Japanese equities and the broader cost-push inflation picture.

**KOSPI — ~6,388.47** | Bias: Bullish
The KOSPI surged 2.72% to 6,388.47 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], the strongest daily performer in today's data bundle. South Korea's benchmark rose approximately 2% alongside Taiwan's Taiex as Asian markets embraced the prospect of peace talks, with the NYT noting these markets have outsized sensitivity to energy prices given large net oil imports [Source: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/21/business/oil-stocks-gas-iran.html].

**ASX 200 — ~8,949.40** | Bias: Neutral
The ASX 200 was essentially flat, declining 0.04% to 8,949.40 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], reflecting Australia's mixed position as a commodity exporter benefiting from high energy prices but also facing headwinds from global growth concerns. The strong AUD may be providing a modest drag on export-sensitive sectors.

### Europe

**DAX — ~24,569.83** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
The DAX gained 0.62% to 24,569.83 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], outperforming US markets as European equities benefit from reduced energy disruption fears and a broadly de-risking narrative. The Stoxx 600 also inched up 0.5% on Tuesday [Source: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/21/business/oil-stocks-gas-iran.html]. ECB policy remains cautious with ING noting that "surveys and ECB stance cap upside" for the euro [Source: https://www.bitget.com/amp/news/detail/12560605376249], which paradoxically supports European equities through a weaker EUR channel.

## 6. CRYPTOCURRENCY

**BTC — ~$76,511.00** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- Bitcoin recovered to $76,511 (+0.84%) today [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], demonstrating notable resilience to the weekend Iran escalation. CoinDesk reported BTC only slipped to $74,335 at the weekend selloff lows despite a 5.7% Brent surge, and observed that "shrinking bitcoin sell-offs with each Iran shock suggest crypto may have largely priced in geopolitical tail risk" [Source: https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2026/04/20/bitcoin-ether-solana-slide-oil-jumps-on-renewed-u-s-iran-war-risks]. Strategy (MSTR) added 34,164 BTC for $2.54 billion last week at an average of $74,395, its third-largest purchase on record, providing a structural bid [Source: https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2026/04/20/strategy-buys-34-164-bitcoin-for-usd2-54-billion].

- ETH gained 0.34% to $2,322.97 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], broadly tracking BTC's recovery but with less momentum. Ethereum continues to underperform Bitcoin on a relative basis in the current macro environment where BTC's narrative as a geopolitical hedge is stronger.
- Key levels: $2,150 support | $2,500 resistance
- Catalyst: Bitcoin directional leadership; ETF flow data; upcoming Ethereum protocol upgrades

- XRP outperformed with a 1.06% gain to $1.44 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], the strongest performer in the crypto basket today. XRP's relative strength may reflect renewed optimism around regulatory clarity and institutional payment network adoption following the broader post-war de-risking of crypto assets.
- Key levels: $1.30 support | $1.60 resistance
- Catalyst: SEC regulatory developments; Ripple partnerships; broader crypto risk appetite

**SOL — ~$85.92** | Bias: Neutral
- Solana gained 0.69% to $85.92 [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/], recovering modestly from weekend lows when it slid alongside BTC and ETH on Iran escalation. The spot ETF bid and improving macro backdrop provide tactical support.

- Catalyst: Solana network activity and DeFi volumes; BTC price leadership; regulatory environment

## 8. WEEK AHEAD / SCENARIO ANALYSIS

| SCENARIO | PROBABILITY | TRIGGER | BRENT | S&P 500 | BTC |
|----------|-------------|---------|-------|---------|-----|
| Bull | 30% | US–Iran peace talks produce durable ceasefire; Hormuz fully reopens; Warsh confirms Fed independence; Tesla/Boeing beat with strong guidance | $82 | 7,350 | $82,000 |
| Base | 50% | Ceasefire talks make incremental progress but Hormuz remains partially restricted; Warsh hearing produces no surprises; earnings broadly meet expectations | $90 | 7,100 | $77,000 |
| Bear | 20% | Iran retaliates militarily against USS Spruance or US assets; Hormuz fully closed again; Warsh signals aggressive balance sheet reduction, spooking bonds; key earnings disappoint | $100 | 6,750 | $68,000 |

**Key Upcoming Catalysts:**

1. **Kevin Warsh Fed Confirmation Hearing (this week)** — Market-moving potential on Fed independence, balance sheet, and rate cut trajectory. Bond market and USD highly sensitive. [Source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-19/kevin-warsh-fed-hearings-will-be-treasury-bonds-next-catalyst]

2. **Tesla Q1 Earnings (Tuesday, April 21)** — Key sentiment indicator for Nasdaq and AI/EV narrative; Jefferies has raised PT to $350. [Source: https://www.tipranks.com/news/these-are-the-questions-that-tesla-tsla-will-answer-during-its-q1-earnings-call-this-week]

3. **Boeing Q1 Earnings (Wednesday, April 22)** — Following a 57% sales jump last quarter; guidance on order book and supply chain disruption critical. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/19/earnings-playbook-tsla-ba-lead-a-big-week-of-reports.html]

4. **Bank Indonesia Rate Decision (Wednesday, April 22)** — All 31 Reuters-surveyed economists expect a hold at 4.75%; forward guidance on inflation and Hormuz impact will set tone for EM FX. [Source: https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/bank-indonesia-hold-rates-475-through-2026-iran-war-fuels-inflation-risks-2026-04-20/]

5. **Intel Q1 Earnings (Thursday, April 23)** — After a 13% post-guidance plunge last quarter; flat YoY revenue expected, but any semiconductor demand guidance for H2 will move the Nasdaq. [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/19/earnings-playbook-tsla-ba-lead-a-big-week-of-reports.html]

---

### Disputed

- [Report states "DXY at 98.221" and describes it as reflecting "broad dollar weakness"]: The DXY is actually **up 0.17%** on the day per the data bundle, not declining. A rising DXY is inconsistent with the "broad dollar weakness" narrative used to explain EUR/USD and GBP/USD gains. The report conflates the DXY's absolute level (~98) — which is historically moderate — with directional weakness, but the intraday move is actually a dollar *gain*, not a loss. This is a directional error in the narrative.

- [Report claims the Guardian article shows WTI surged to $90.17 on Sunday, then states current WTI is $87.32 (-2.56%), while Brent is $90.45 (-5.27%)]: The Sunday Brent surge to $96.27 and the current -5.27% close at $90.45 are arithmetically inconsistent as a single-day
